How do we end my last tape here before I go live? You know I'm psyched to go live or I could do your Q&As and everything. So here's the deal. I picked a topic here that I really thought was interesting and that I thought would really be a great way to kind of sum up everything that we've accomplished and also why the straight line works so well, why you're getting the results that you're getting. I think it's really going to shed some light on that. So what I call this training here, I've come up with a name for it, it's called the overarching equations of the straight line. And I've mentioned this to you many times in passing, you know, I say this is overarching equations, I say like in the straight line, like what it does basically, it takes all these things that are operating behind the scenes. Like all these things that for someone like myself that's like a natural born world class closer, like all these things that are going on in my head that automatically get coagulated and spit out the right way. What the straight line basically does, it takes in all these rules into account and then allows you to essentially operate within that universe of rules. These are crucial things that essentially must happen to influence someone. So the straight line essentially operates within these boundaries of like all these rules and allows you to move forward from lack of certainty to absolute certainty without breaking any of the rules. Because if you break any of the rules, right, what happens? Well, then you can't close. And there's, you have to be in a state of certainty. You're absolutely certain about your product. And then what you do is you transfer that certainty to the prospect using the straight line. So the perfect analogy here would be that essentially imagine you have a home, right? So in the home, you have a furnace, right? And the furnace creates heat. But how does the heat get to the rest of the rooms in the house? Well, you have an HVAC, some ductwork, right? And the ductwork is what allows the heat to flow from a furnace through the various parts of the house that don't need to be heated. So it elegantly flows to the individual places. It directs the flow of the energy of the heat and warms up the room. So in this case, in the metaphor, the straight line system is like the ductwork. It's the HVAC system and you are the furnace. So if the furnace is not lit up, if the furnace is cold, what happens? I don't care how great the piping might be the most efficient piping in the world, right? Perfect. No leaks. It's just right. Nothing to transfer. So what happens is in the real world, we can't just focus on success, meaning to master the tactics and techniques of the straight line. And with that, we want to number two. Okay. And this is a great one. Now let's get into the nitty gritty here, right? One of the things I say about the straight line, I have this great example I use and they talk about the two types of selling. And this is one of the great things about the idiocy of a lot of the sales training programs that are out there. They focus on like two things. They say, you know, there's the old sales model, which is like that hard charging, right? Graham by the throat and close them model, right? Called the old model. And then they have the new way of selling, which is a consultative soft approach. So let me explain to you what those two models are and we'll see what's what. Right. So in the old model of selling, okay, you have that's the hard charging sort of like the perceived, like the Glenn, Gary, Glenn, Gary, Glenn, rest of Wolf of Walsh. You'd be like, just, I just ripped their throats out. Right. In that model, what they say is you want to spend basically 10% of your time building rapport, right? 20% of your time. I'm not just, you know, how shitty I am with this stuff with all this, right? 20% of your time identifying a qualifying, right? 30% of your time, okay, presenting. And this should be 40% right? And 40% of your time closing. In other words, the bulk of what you're doing in the old model is you're just a hard charging closing machine who pays very little attention to getting, you know, you're getting to report and that's it. You're done. Right. And this is what it's all about. Close, close, close, close, close. ABC always be closed. That's the old model of selling, right? And then we, on the other side of the equation have what's called the new model, or the consultative model. And what they do here is they kind of flip things that upside its head. They say it's 40% of what you do is about building relationships. 40% is building relationships, which is basically another name for getting into a report people. They say you want to spend 30% of the time identifying needs, which is qualifying. They want to say you have 20% of your time offering solutions and 10% of your time closing, confirming and closing. That's the consultative model. And I have to tell you that this is so idiotically ridiculous, so ridiculous. I mean, if you spend 10% of your time closing, you'll be broke and you'll be out of the game. You can't do that because the fact is you're always closing. But here's the other part that's ironic is that this model is just as stupid. Okay. Here's the truth. And here's what the straight line is about. With the straight line system, you're always doing all things at once. You're not getting into report 10% 20. You always have to be building rapport. Remember, rapport is not a constant. If at any point during a sale, you slip out of rapport, you're screwed. You can't influence, right? So in other words, rapport, they say one says 10%. The other one says 40%. I'll take it's 100%. You're always building rapport, maintaining rapport. And you have almost an eagle eye saying if you feel yourself sliding out of rapport, you need to jump in and fix that shit right away and get yourself back in rapport because you can't move forward on the straight line without being in rapport. It's just, it's a must. Right? Second aspect, asking questions, gathering intelligence, identifying needs. Whatever you want to call it, either model, right? You never stop asking questions. In other words, sales is about asking questions and identifying solutions based on those questions you ask. But the point is, it's not like I'm going to ask questions for this little part and I'm never going to know. Remember, looping, what are we doing? We're constantly asking people questions. Does it make sense to you? We never stop. We're always asking questions. So again, when it comes to identifying needs or asking questions, we're doing that 100% of the time as well. Never stops, right? Presenting our case or presenting our solution, right? Guess what? Okay. With the old model, I think it was a 30 and then it's 20 with the base, which one? Yeah. Right? Here's the deal. Ready? With the straight line, 100% of the time you're offering solutions from the first word out of your mouth. That's your solution. You know why you're an expert and experts are the one that give people solutions. It's not like I can just sort of like kind of be all the girl kind of rambling and not be presenting and kind of have this, I got a free time where I kind of free form and then only in this defined moment in the preso. At the heart of every decision that we make as human beings is something called the law of positive intent. In other words, human beings, we do things. We decide because we think our decision will enhance our lives in someone. We don't do things that we think will make our life worse. You follow me? We say yes. We think it will make our life better. And we'll say no, we think it'll make it worse. Or we will say yes, we think it will cause us pleasure. And we say no, we think it will cause us pain. But what does that mean with the straight line? Here's a great one. Right. Now, four or five months ago, I was walking on the beach and I was doing a training on looping and I came up with this, like kind of this statement, certainty wanes. And I've said that many times before, certainty wanes, but I started using that as a way of explaining why we loop. And in fact, I call it certainty rain wanes, which is why we like hold that's right. How certainty wanes, which is why we loop. So let me go through this because this is again, is another overarching equations. Just it's human nature. And because certainty wanes, no, explain what that means in a second. That's why we loop. What I mean by certainty wanes is that you give a awesome presentation to someone. Right. I mean, just get it out of the park. Right. And you sum up all the benefits and your future pace. And then when you're done, you did such a good job and you say, well, let me ask you, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? They say, oh my, yes, you're like, I'm excited. Yeah, it's great. It's a perfect group. I love the idea. They're like at a 9.5 or 10. Right. That's, that's their apps. You've gotten into a state of absolute certainty. Right. Four minutes later, they're back at 8.5. They, the certainty drops, it wanes, it doesn't last ever. The only thing that lasts forever is a conviction. And that's a very different thing. A conviction is a tacit belief. Like you're so fucking sure that it's unshakable and never drops, but it takes a long time for someone to get to a point. It's a conviction and it's very, very hard to do it in interest sale, so to speak. Right. Example, right now I have a conviction that I'm a great salesperson. No one's going to shake that belief. I know I am. And my, excuse me, it's right or wrong. In my heart and my mind, I'm sure, right? Here's another conviction, more appropriate. I think that iPhones are the best. You know, no one's going to convince me. I mean, I'm not going to be convinced this point to switch to Samsung. And by the way, they're great too. I know that Samsung's a good news for people for them. For me, I love my iPhones. I'm sure of it. That's what I buy. It's what I use. Same thing goes with my car. Drive from Mercedes. I'm convinced I'm sure of that. It took me many years to get there, but now I'm sure that's a 10 on the certainty scale. You get them to a 9.6 where they're almost there. But as soon as you stop talking and those seeds of doubt start creeping back in, they start to fall. Certainty wins, which is why when I loop. Here's where you do your intelligence gathering report building, right? And we do all things at all times. Of course, we make our presentation. We ask for the order for the first time and let's say when you do it, they say they want to think about you say, well, this is the idea makes sense to do. You like the idea. They say that sounds pretty good. Right. So that's the basis for your first loop. You're going to loop back. Okay. And you're going to resell the idea and then you'll say, again, does that makes you see it now? They say, oh, yeah, it's great. It's awesome. So that's your first, first part of your loop. Right. So you're like, check. You got them to, let's say a nine on the certainty scale. Then you say, well, let me ask you this. Then you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. This is called your what? Your forest, gump bag, right? And you're going to resell yourself. Right. And as you're doing that, you're reselling yourself. Guess what's happening? As honest as it seems, you're building up certainty for yourself. And this is already going, it's already starting to do a dive. I bet not a nose dive, just subtle drop off to a nine to an 8.7 certainty. Wains. Someone says, oh yeah, I love the idea. And then, and like a microsecond later, the seeds of top, wait a second. But I really like, they doubt themselves how we are, we doubt ourselves. Right. So what happens is, is as you're running a loop, you're almost like finding two forces at once, you'll be, you're fighting the battle of whatever 10 you're focused on in that moment. And you're also aware that the previous 10 is starting to slip. So what that means is at the very end, before we close, we'll do one last button up of each of the three 10s. We button things up one last time. And then we ask for the order. This is a crucial aspect to succeeding with the straight line. In other words, we remember that because certainty wanes, we're always, it's like we're fighting this like this, like the decaying force of certainty that naturally occurs. You didn't do anything wrong. It's just how human beings are built. So that's why that's an overarching equation is because that force, that natural force of nature is why the straight line operates the way it operates. That's why we loop back and we keep reinforcing, reselling the first 10, the second 10, the third 10. We don't just say, check, I got the first 10 and resolve. I don't have to talk about that again. No, when we run our next loop, we're going to go back and resell the first 10 again, not as in depth, but we need to button back up. Cause what happens is, let's say we got into a 9.5 and now naturally we're running on a second, they dropped back down to an 8.5. I want to make sure I hit this again and bring them back up to a 9.7 even a little higher before I move forward on the line. You have to go back and reinforce what you did. And when you start doing that, when you start really understanding that you're always sort of fighting this decay of, it's the natural decay of certainty. It gives you the sort of the context to realize why you run the patterns the way you run them, why you always sum things up one last time at the end. It's because you're a buttoning up certainty for everything, for all three 10s. All right. It's an important overarching equation and it governs whether or not you get a close or not. All right. Let's move forward to the next one here. Pick a good one here. Got a bunch. Ah, here's the, okay. Here's the biggest one of all, right? Okay. This is the equation and I've mentioned this to you a couple of times and I don't think I've really ever dug into it as deeply as I'd like. And this is what's called your energy in benefits out equation. All right. And to do this, I'm going to tell you a really funny story. All right. Let me tell you a story that will drive this home. Many years ago, I had a problem with my tooth, right? I had this, like even in college, I had an infection. They drilled. Anyway, I tried over the years to save this tooth and did for like 10, 12 years. And I kept getting a root canal and then another root canal and then this and that. And eventually it got to the point where the tooth had to go, right? It just had to go, right? So I'll go to the dentist, right? And he yanks out this tooth, right? And I'm going to now a space now. And now I have to go to make a decision of what kind of, you know, implant I want. I want a regular full blown implant or do I want like what's called a bridge, two options. And the dentist starts to explain to me about, okay, here's what it is to give an implant. And here's how you go about getting a bridge. And he explains that with the bridge, okay, you come here and I'll create some sort of like, you know, artificial thing and it hooks to the back tooth, to the front tooth. And I put it on, right? And simple as that. And you're done, right? It goes, the only problem with that is a bridge doesn't really last forever. It's kind of like, you know, it could crack when you're eating one day. It's not like sort of like, you know, you'll always be a little bit conscious that you have a bridge. The other alternative is you can go get a full blown dental implant. Because now if you get an implant, okay, here's what has to happen. You're going to come in now, it takes you three or four trips to the dentist. I'm like, oh my fucking, I'm like three or four trips to the dentist, right? He goes, and in the first trip I want to do is I have to prepare your bone there because your bones a bit weak. So I'm going to put some pigs, fucking bone, like what? Like pigs bone, who I couldn't believe it. We'll put some stuff there to help make your bone tighter and strong, right? Because then once it's there, you'll come back and we're going to have to send you to the oral surgeon who's got a screw and implant in there. Like put a screw in there. Then we have to wait three to four months till that screw sets, right? Once that screw is fully set in there, okay, then we'll come back and we get it finally fit you for this tooth here and put this to the screw, this tooth back in. And once it's there, it's permanent. I'm like, what the fuck? I'm like four trips to the dentist. And I can't even believe this. Now, just so you know, I ended up going and getting an implant, but not from this dentist. I said, let me think about it. That's, I said, and when I went home, did I think I didn't think about shit. I'm like, this is crazy. What the guy did. Okay. Is he essentially created a scenario for me where I had to expend all this type of energy and a goat back to the dentist to the oral surgeon under anesthesia, back to the dentist, back to the, so it was a one year process. It caused a implant will last forever. And I just, I couldn't, I couldn't, in my own mind, I had to expend too much energy. And then when he told me the price, you know, an implant will cost you four grand. A bridge is only $800. So I'm thinking I should take the bridge. I mean, like, but here's the deal. The truth is, is that the implant is much better for you. Implant is a tooth you'll have for a lifetime. Much better. You want the implant, right? But the way this guy positioned it, the way he explained it, I'm looking at this is massive energy expenditure on the way in. I got to keep going back to the dentist, getting in the car, driving there, wasting my time. And then of course money is the ultimate form of stored energy, right? You work, you spend your money to live, to your food, shelter, clothing, right? The excess you have, you put in the bank, that's stored energy from your hard work. And then you can essentially deploy that energy, deploy the stored energy, how you see fit. So when you do that, when you deploy that energy, right? Well, you know, that energy had a cost of your hard work to it, right? So I want to make sure that when I deploy energy, I direct energy that the benefits on the other side are much greater. So imagine if you set up a closing scenario, so the guy explains to me how to hold teeth, the whole thing, my options, and he sends up the scenario. And to me, it sounds like I have to put in all this fricking energy, spend a lot of money to get the implant. And he didn't really explain to me why the implant was better. What's the benefits out? Now, if this guy knew even this much about the straight line system, what he would have done is said, listen, it's very simple. It's a few trips to the doctor. It's a bit of a pain. Yes, no big deal. Okay, we make you comfortable. You come in, we'll put the screw in here. You're under no pain. There's no pain felt at all. Okay. And we'll give you a temporary tooth in there so it doesn't look bad while you're waiting. And once it's all done, you'll know you have a tooth for life. You'll be able to smile freely, eat any type of food you want. And when you're 65, 70 years old, trust me, you'll be glad you had this. It's only $5,000. Now there's a buy signal is when you ask for the order and rather than getting an objection or a yes, you get what's called a buy signal. Say, how much does that cost again? That's a buy signal. So far. We'll get something to me. Two more here. Okay. Um, here's another great one here. Okay. It has to do with about, is an over-aughting equation about never letting your guard down. Here's the deal. We judge books by their covers, right? We make snap decisions. We see someone, we take them in, we rip each piece of them apart, and then we put them back together based on how they're perceived. If we perceive someone as being sharp as attack, enthusiastic, a silent expert in the field, right? It opens us up to say, you know what? This person could probably help us achieve our goals, help us get what we want. So we listen. We defer, we essentially give control to those who are experts in their field. Right? That's a kind of obvious thing. It's the basis of the straight line. It's why we must capture their attention in those first four seconds in exactly the right way. And if you do that, it allows us to take control of the conversation and move them slowly, but surely step, step down the straight line, right? Basic, right? But here's the point where people fall down. They perfect the art of sounding really good in those first four seconds. And even for the 10 seconds after that, right? But imagine, remember, you have a long sale here. You know, how long does the sale take? Well, I can't really tell you, because all of your businesses are different. But maybe it takes somewhere between eight and 30 minutes, right? To make a sale. Here's the deal. This is, this is the almost the full overwatching question. The whole thing. If at any point along this continuum, you are revealed as a novice, you're screwed, you can never let your guard down. There is no amount of expert currency where you could build, like you build up so much currency that you're an expert that now you could say stupid shit. It never works. There's one reveal. The human mind is lightning fast. Even one reveal that wait a second, things are not as they seem. There's something about this person. There's an incongruency. It's enough to, to like to poison the sale. So when I talk about these first four seconds, right? Then you must capture someone's attention, position yourself sharp as it's not just the exact enthusiasm, an expert in your field, right? I don't just mean then that you can let your guard down and be average, be okay. You can never be perceived as anything less than a total expert in your field. Remember how it works. In the first four seconds, it's a gut reaction, right? You know, someone perceives you the right way. It's a gut reaction they have, right? Fast and they can think it's not the conscious mind, it's an unconscious mind based on a pattern. That says, you know what? This is how experts sound. This is what experts say. This is how experts look and because that you're perceived the right way. Right? That's an unconscious reaction lightning fast. But then what happens is the conscious mind now takes over and it starts saying, Hmm, is this, is this congruent? In other words, it starts to almost like it's the, the conscious mind is the inspector is looking for some, is there, is my being lied to that someone trying to pull the wool over my eyes. The conscious mind is very suspicious. The unconscious mind is not suspicious. It just bam, it makes a decision. You have all this information stored away since you were a big, your conscious mind remembers everything. And based on the patterns that you formed and laid down, it looks for similarities. It says when someone is dressed this way, sounds this way and acts way, they fall into what the expert bucket that's, and it puts you in the expert bucket. No thought required. But as a self-protective mechanism now, as the sale progresses, their conscious mind, they're either looking, they're looking for that one inconsistent, they have one incongruency to say, ah, it wasn't real. Knowing that it means that you have to basically have the entire sale mapped out from front to back and that gets to the main and final point, which is it's at the end of the day, what really drives a straight line is strategic preparation. Strategic prep, meaning that before you enter any sales encounter, you need to map out in your mind and on paper, right? All the possible eventualities, all the scenarios and have answers and patterns and scripts that allow you to go from open to close all the way through and give yourself as many options as possible. In other words, it's not enough just to have one aspect of the screen. You need to have the whole thing locked down from front to back because at any point in time, remember, and here's another archery, I sort of mentioned this for another archery, overarching law, is that rapport is not a constant, right? You could be in total rapport and once you break it, it's gone. It's not like you're in rapport and now I'm good now. No, there's no constant. You can be ultra tight rapport and you say something and breaks it and you can't close. So in other words, it's running the entire length of the, when I say it's an overarching, it means it runs the entire length of the straight line. You always need to be in rapport, which means you're not going to always be in rapport, which means if you fall out, you have to get back in. So in other words, you can't move forward on the line when you're out of rapport. You just can't. So this whole idea that you're perceived the right way and that opens you up to taking control and when you take control, when you ask questions and you use your active listening to get into that ultra tight rapport with the, uh-huh. Well, that right. You get into that ultra tight rapport, but once you're there, it's not like, okay, check rapport is done. I could be an asshole now at any point in time based on the last words that you said. And when the prospect agreed with you or disagree with you, whether they resonated with it or didn't resonate with it, right? That rapport will kind of go up and down. It varies. And if you say something stupid or something offensive or something that reveals you for being a novice, the rapport falls off a cliff and you can't close. And you're revealed as a novice. And for all the other reasons you get it, it's basically a death spiral. All right. So when I talk about the straight line, why it works so well at the heart of it are these overarching essentially the human, the human condition basically, these are just how people are built, how we operate, how we perceive reality. So the straight line essentially allows you to go from the open to the close without violating these fundamental rules that human beings go by when they make decisions. So I want you to go through this again, this train when you can really review again and again.
